name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا يَحِلُّ لَكُمْ أَن تَرِثُوا۟ ٱلنِّسَآءَ كَرْهًا ۖ وَلَا تَعْضُلُوهُنَّ لِتَذْهَبُوا۟ بِبَعْضِ مَآ ءَاتَيْتُمُوهُنَّ إِلَّآ أَن يَأْتِينَ بِفَـٰحِشَةٍ مُّبَيِّنَةٍ ۚ وَعَاشِرُوهُنَّ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ ۚ فَإِن كَرِهْتُمُوهُنَّ فَعَسَىٰٓ أَن تَكْرَهُوا۟ شَيْـًٔا وَيَجْعَلَ ٱللَّهُ فِيهِ خَيْرًا كَثِيرًا (١٩)
```

```english
O you who have faith! It is not lawful for you to inherit women forcibly, and do not press them to take away part of what you have given them, unless they commit a gross indecency. Consort with them in an honourable manner; and should you dislike them, maybe you dislike something while Allah invests it with an abundant good. (19)
```

```arabic
وَإِنْ أَرَدتُّمُ ٱسْتِبْدَالَ زَوْجٍ مَّكَانَ زَوْجٍ وَءَاتَيْتُمْ إِحْدَىٰهُنَّ قِنطَارًا فَلَا تَأْخُذُوا۟ مِنْهُ شَيْـًٔا ۚ أَتَأْخُذُونَهُۥ بُهْتَـٰنًا وَإِثْمًا مُّبِينًا (٢٠)
```

```english
If you desire to take a wife in place of another, and you have given one of them a quintal [of gold], do not take anything away from it. Would you take it by way of calumny and flagrant sin?! (20)
```

```arabic
وَكَيْفَ تَأْخُذُونَهُۥ وَقَدْ أَفْضَىٰ بَعْضُكُمْ إِلَىٰ بَعْضٍ وَأَخَذْنَ مِنكُم مِّيثَـٰقًا غَلِيظًا (٢١)
```

```english
How could you take it back, when you have known each other, and they have taken from you a solemn covenant? (21)
```

```arabic
وَلَا تَنكِحُوا۟ مَا نَكَحَ ءَابَآؤُكُم مِّنَ ٱلنِّسَآءِ إِلَّا مَا قَدْ سَلَفَ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ فَـٰحِشَةً وَمَقْتًا وَسَآءَ سَبِيلًا (٢٢)
```

```english
Do not marry any of the women whom your fathers had married, excluding what is already past. That is indeed an indecency, an outrage and an evil course. (22)
```

## (Commentary)

This is a return to the matter of women by mentioning other regulations concerning them. Alongside this, the verses contain His saying: **وَعاشِرُوهُنَّ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ فَإِنْ كَرِهْتُمُوهُنَّ فَعَسى أَنْ تَكْرَهُوا شَيْئاً وَيَجْعَلَ اللهُ فِيهِ خَيْراً كَثِيراً** — "And consort with them in a good manner; and if you should be averse to them, it may be that you are averse to something and Allah creates in it much good." (4:19). Indeed, this is a Quranic foundation for the social life of women.

His saying, the Exalted: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا يَحِلُّ لَكُمْ** — "O you who have faith! It is not lawful for you..." up to His saying: **كَرْهاً** — "...forcibly." (4:19). The people of the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية) — according to history and narration — used to count the wives of the deceased as part of the inheritance (التركة), provided the woman was not the mother of the heir. Thus, they would inherit them along with the estate. One of the heirs would cast a garment over the wife of the deceased and thereby inherit her. If he wished, he would marry her without a dower (مهر), but rather by right of inheritance; and if he was averse to marrying her, he would detain her with him. Then, if he wished, he would marry her off to someone else and benefit from her dower, or if he wished, he would constrain her (عضلها) and prevent her from marriage, keeping her detained until she died so that he might inherit her if she possessed wealth.

Although the apparent meaning of the verse is that it forbids a practice circulating among them—which is the inheritance of women that we mentioned—and is thus intended to deter them from this evil custom as some exegetes have stated, His saying at the end of the sentence, **كَرْهاً** — "forcibly," does not align with that, whether it is taken as an explanatory (توضيحي) or a restrictive (احترازي) qualification.

For if it were an explanatory qualification, it would imply that this inheritance always occurs against the will of the women, which is not the case, as is obvious. And if it were a restrictive qualification, it would imply that the prohibition only applies when the inheritance is against the women's will, but not when it is with their consent, which is also not the case.

Rather, "force" (الكره) is a reality that manifests in the constraint (العضل) from marriage out of greed for their inheritance, always or mostly, after seizing them through inheritance. Thus, it appears that the verse is in the position of deterring this inheritance against their will. As for marrying them by right of inheritance, that which addresses its prohibition is the saying of the Exalted that follows: **وَلا تَنْكِحُوا ما نَكَحَ آباؤُكُمْ مِنَ النِّساءِ** — "Do not marry women whom your fathers married..." (4:22). As for marrying them off to others and taking their dower, this is forbidden by verses such as the saying of the Exalted: **وَلِلنِّساءِ نَصِيبٌ مِمَّا اكْتَسَبْنَ** — "...and to women belongs a share of what they have earned." (4:32). All of this is indicated by the saying of the Exalted: **فَلا جُناحَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِيما فَعَلْنَ فِي أَنْفُسِهِنَّ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ** — "...there is no sin upon you in what they may do with themselves in an honorable manner." (2:234).

As for His subsequent saying: **وَلا تَعْضُلُوهُنَّ لِتَذْهَبُوا** — "and do not constrain them that you may take away..." (4:19) etc., this is different from the aforementioned constraint from marriage to take wealth through inheritance. This is because the concluding phrase, **لِتَذْهَبُوا بِبَعْضِ ما آتَيْتُمُوهُنَّ** — "...that you may take away part of what you have given them," indicates that what is meant is taking away part of the dower that the constraining husband had given, rather than wealth she owned through other means. In short, the verse forbids the inheritance of women's wealth against their will, rather than the inheritance of their persons; thus, the attribution of inheritance to "women" (النساء) is by assuming "wealth" (الأموال) as the elided object, or it is a rational metaphor (مجاز عقلي).

His saying, the Exalted: **وَلا تَعْضُلُوهُنَّ لِتَذْهَبُوا** — "and do not constrain them that you may take away..." up to His saying: **مُبَيِّنَةٍ** — "...manifest." (4:19). This is either coordinated with His saying: **تَرِثُوا** — "that you should inherit," with the implied meaning being: "nor that you should constrain them"; or it is a prohibition coordinated with His saying: **لا يَحِلُّ لَكُمْ** — "it is not lawful for you," because the latter carries the meaning of a prohibition. Constraint (العضل) is prevention, restriction, and harshness. Indecency (الفاحشة) is a loathsome way of conduct, and its usage is frequent regarding adultery (الزنا). Manifest (المبينة) means that which is clear (المتبينة). It has been narrated from Sibawayh that *abana* (أبان), *istabana* (استبان), *bayyana* (بين), and *tabayyana* (تبين) all carry the same meaning, being both transitive and intransitive.

The verse forbids restricting them in any manner of restriction so that they are forced to relinquish part of the dower to dissolve the marriage bond and escape the hardship of life. Restricting them with this intention is forbidden for the husband, unless the wife commits a manifest indecency (فاحشة مبينة); in that case, he may constrain her and restrict her so that she separates from him by paying a ransom (البذل). This verse does not contradict the other verse regarding ransom: **وَلا يَحِلُّ لَكُمْ أَنْ تَأْخُذُوا مِمَّا آتَيْتُمُوهُنَّ شَيْئاً إِلَّا أَنْ يَخافا أَلَّا يُقِيما حُدُودَ اللهِ فَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ أَلَّا يُقِيما حُدُودَ اللهِ فَلا جُناحَ عَلَيْهِما فِيمَا افْتَدَتْ بِهِ** — "It is not lawful for you to take back anything from what you have given them, unless the couple fears that they may not maintain Allah’s bounds; so if you fear they may not maintain Allah’s bounds, there is no sin upon them in what she may give to ransom herself." (2:229). Rather, it is a specification (تخصيص). This verse specifies the verse of *al-Baqarah* to the case of committing indecency. As for the ransom mentioned in the verse of *al-Baqarah*, it occurs based on mutual consent, so this verse is not specified by it.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَعاشِرُوهُنَّ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ** — "And consort with them in a good manner" (4:19) until the end of the verse. The "good manner" (المعروف) is that which people recognize in their society without denying or being ignorant of it. Since the command to consort is qualified by it, the meaning is the command to consort with them in the manner recognized among those being commanded.

The consorting that men recognize and practice among themselves is that each one of them is a constitutive part of society, equal to other parts in forming the human society for the purpose of cooperation and general mutual support of the species. Thus, a duty is directed toward each of them to strive to the best of his ability for what the society needs; he acquires what benefits him, gives what he can dispense with, and takes what he requires. If one of the parts of society were treated otherwise, it would be nothing but oppression by nullifying his independence as a part, making him a follower who is utilized without receiving anything equivalent in return; and this is the exception.

Allah, the Exalted, has clarified in His Book that all people—men and women—are branches of a single human root, and parts and portions of a single human nature. Society, in its formation, is in need of these just as it is in need of those, on an equal footing, as He, the Exalted, said: **بَعْضُكُمْ مِنْ بَعْضٍ** — "...you are all [members] of one another." (4:25).

This does not contradict the fact that each of the two groups is characterized by a specific trait, such as men being characterized by toughness and strength (الشدة والقوة) as a type, and women being characterized by tenderness and emotion (الرقة والعاطفة) by nature. For human nature, in both its ontological and social life, requires the manifestation of toughness and strength just as it requires the flow of affection and mercy. Both traits are manifestations of the general attraction and repulsion within human society.

Thus, the two groups are balanced in weight and impact, just as the individuals within the group of men are equal in weight and influence in this composite structure, despite their differences in natural and social affairs—such as strength and weakness, knowledge and ignorance, shrewdness and dullness, youth and old age, leadership and subordination, being served and serving, nobility and baseness, and so on.

This is the judgment that springs from the taste of a moderate society that follows the path of the primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — without deviation. Islam has straightened the pillars of human society and corrected its crookedness; thus, there is no escape from the application of the rule of equality in consorting, which we express as social freedom (الحرية الاجتماعية). The freedom of women is like that of men, and its reality is that a human being, qua human, possesses thought and will, and has the right to choose what benefits him over what harms him, being independent in his choice. Then, when he enters society, he has the right to choose what he chooses—so long as it does not conflict with the happiness of human society—being independent in that without being prevented or forced to follow another without choice.

This, as you have realized, does not contradict the specialization of certain classes or certain individuals within a single class with privileges, or their deprivation of privileges—such as the specialization of men in Islam with the judiciary, governance, and struggle (الجهاد), and the obligation of providing maintenance (النفقة) for women upon men, and so on. Similarly, the deprivation of non-pubescent children from the validity of their acknowledgments and transactions, and the fact that religious obligations are not directed toward them, and the like. All of these are specificities of laws that apply to classes and individuals of society based on their differing weights within society, after they all share in the fundamental human social weight, the criterion of which is that everyone is a human being possessing thought and will.

These specificities are not unique to the sacred law of Islam; rather, they are found in all civil laws, and indeed in all human customs, even the primitive ones, to a greater or lesser extent. The comprehensive word for all these meanings is the saying of the Exalted: **وَعاشِرُوهُنَّ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ** — "And consort with them in a good manner," as has been clarified.

As for His saying, the Exalted: **فَإِنْ كَرِهْتُمُوهُنَّ فَعَسى أَنْ تَكْرَهُوا شَيْئاً وَيَجْعَلَ اللهُ فِيهِ خَيْراً كَثِيراً** — "...and if you should be averse to them, it may be that you are averse to something and Allah creates in it much good" (4:19), it is of the nature of presenting a known matter in the form of something doubtful and possible, to avoid awakening the instinct of prejudice in the addressee. This is similar to His saying, the Exalted: **قُلْ مَنْ يَرْزُقُكُمْ مِنَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ قُلِ اللهُ وَإِنَّا أَوْ إِيَّاكُمْ لَعَلى هُدىً أَوْ فِي ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ قُلْ لا تُسْئَلُونَ عَمَّا أَجْرَمْنا وَلا نُسْئَلُ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ** — "Say, ‘Who provides for you from the heavens and the earth?’ Say, ‘Allah! Indeed either we or you are rightly guided or in manifest error.’ Say, ‘You will not be questioned about our crimes, nor shall we be questioned about what you do.’" (34:24-25).

For human society at that time (the era of the Quran's revelation) did not place women in their true human position, and was averse to her entering society as a constitutive part. Rather, the societies standing at that time were divided between those who considered them external parasites whose existence is utilized, and those who considered them humans deficient in humanity, like children and the insane, except that they would never reach full humanity; thus, they must always live under subordination and domination. Perhaps His saying, the Exalted: **فَإِنْ كَرِهْتُمُوهُنَّ** — "if you should be averse to them," where He attributed the aversion to their persons rather than to their marriage, is an allusion to this.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِنْ أَرَدْتُمُ اسْتِبْدالَ زَوْجٍ مَكانَ زَوْجٍ** — "And if you desire to replace a wife with [another] wife..." (4:20) until the end of the verse. Replacement (الاستبدال) is the seeking of a substitute. It is as if it means placing one wife in the position of another, or it is of the nature of inclusion (تضمين) meaning establishing one woman in the place of another through replacement. For this reason, He combined His saying **أَرَدْتُمُ** — "you desire" with **اسْتِبْدالَ** — "replacement," even though replacement already contains the meaning of desire and seeking. Accordingly, the meaning is: "And if you desire to establish one wife in the place of another through replacement."

Slander (البهتان) is that which bewilders (بهت) a person, i.e., makes him confused. Its usage is predominant regarding lies in speech. Originally, it is a verbal noun (مصدر), and it has been used in the verse for the action, which is the taking of the dower. In the verse, it is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for the act of taking, as is His saying: **إِثْماً** — "sinfully." The interrogation is rhetorical/denunciatory (إنكاري).

The meaning is: If you desire to divorce some of your wives and marry others in their place, do not take anything from the dower you have given them, even if what you gave them was a great amount of wealth (قنطاراً) and what you take is very little.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَكَيْفَ تَأْخُذُونَهُ وَقَدْ أَفْضى بَعْضُكُمْ إِلى بَعْضٍ** — "How can you take it back when you have already gone in to each other..." (4:21) until the end of the verse. The interrogation is for expressing wonder (تعجيب). *Al-Ifda’* (الإفضاء) is connection through contact, and its root is *al-fada’* (الفضاء) meaning vastness.

Since this taking is only through transgression and injustice, and its context is one of connection and union, this necessitated the validity of wonder; for the spouses, due to the intimacy and proximity necessitated by marriage, become like a single person. It is wondrous that a single person would wrong himself and hurt it, or that some of his parts would hurt others.

As for His saying: **وَأَخَذْنَ مِنْكُمْ مِيثاقاً غَلِيظاً** — "...and they have taken from you a solemn covenant" (4:21), it appears that what is meant by the "solemn covenant" (الميثاق الغليظ) is the bond that the man solidified through the contract (العقد) and the like, one of the concomitants of which is the dower that is named at the time of marriage and which the woman is entitled to from the man.

It has been said that what is meant by the solemn covenant is the pledge taken from the man for the woman to "retain her honorably or release her kindly," as mentioned by Allah, the Exalted. It has also been said that what is meant is the ruling of lawfulness (الحلية) established by the Sharia in marriage. It is not hidden that both views are far-fetched in relation to the wording of the verse.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, from Hashim ibn Abdillah, from al-Sirri al-Bajali, who said: I asked him about His saying: **وَلا تَعْضُلُوهُنَّ لِتَذْهَبُوا بِبَعْضِ ما آتَيْتُمُوهُنَّ** — "and do not constrain them that you may take away part of what you have given them." He said: He related some words and then said: "As the Nabataeans say: when the garment is cast upon her, he has constrained her (عضلها), so she cannot marry another. This used to be in the Age of Ignorance."

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of the Exalted: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا يَحِلُّ لَكُمْ أَنْ تَرِثُوا النِّساءَ كَرْهاً** — "O you who have faith! It is not lawful for you to inherit women forcibly." He said: "In the Age of Ignorance, during the early period of Islam among the Arab tribes, if a man's close relative died leaving a wife, the man would cast his garment over her and thus inherit her marriage by the dower of his relative—which he had given her. He would inherit her marriage just as he inherited his wealth. When Abu Qays ibn al-Aslat died, Muhsin ibn Abi Qays cast his garment over his father's wife, who was Kubayshah bint Ma'mar ibn Ma'bad, and inherited her marriage. Then he left her, neither entering upon her nor spending on her. So she came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and said: 'O Messenger of Allah, Abu Qays ibn al-Aslat died and Muhsin his son inherited my marriage; he does not enter upon me, nor does he spend on me, nor does he release me so that I may join my family.' The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Return to your house; if Allah reveals anything regarding your matter, I will inform you.' Then there was revealed: **وَلا تَنْكِحُوا ما نَكَحَ آباؤُكُمْ مِنَ النِّساءِ إِلَّا ما قَدْ سَلَفَ إِنَّهُ كانَ فاحِشَةً وَمَقْتاً وَساءَ سَبِيلاً** — 'Do not marry women whom your fathers married, except what has already passed. Indeed it is an indecency and an outrage and an evil course.' (4:22). So she joined her family. There were women in Medina whose marriage had been inherited just as Kubayshah's marriage was inherited, except that they were inherited by the sons. So Allah revealed: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا يَحِلُّ لَكُمْ أَنْ تَرِثُوا النِّساءَ كَرْهاً**."

I say: The end of the narration is not free from some confusion in meaning. This story and the revelation of the verses concerning it have also been reported in several Sunni narrations, except that the narrations—or most of them—mention the revelation of His saying, the Exalted: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا يَحِلُّ لَكُمْ أَنْ تَرِثُوا** in the story. You have already seen in the preceding commentary that the context does not support this.

Nevertheless, the occurrence of the story and the connection of the verses in some way to it and to the custom prevalent among them at the time of revelation is beyond doubt. Thus, what is to be relied upon is what we presented in the preceding commentary.

In *al-Majma'*: Regarding His saying, the Exalted: **إِلَّا أَنْ يَأْتِينَ بِفاحِشَةٍ مُبَيِّنَةٍ** — "...unless they commit a manifest indecency." He said: It is better to interpret the verse as referring to every act of disobedience (معصية). He said: And this is what is narrated from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him).

In *Tafsir al-Burhan*, from al-Shaybani: "Indecency" (الفاحشة) means adultery (الزنا); that is, if the man discovers an indecency from her, he may take a ransom (الفدية). And this is what is narrated from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him).

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Jarir extracted from Jabir that the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "Fear Allah regarding women, for you have taken them by the trust of Allah, and made their private parts lawful by the word of Allah. Your right over them is that they do not allow anyone you dislike to tread upon your beds. If they do that, then strike them with a strike that is not injurious. And their right over you is their provision and clothing in a good manner."

In the same source, Ibn Jarir extracted from Ibn Umar that the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "O people, women are like captives (عوان) with you; you have taken them by the trust of Allah and made their private parts lawful by the word of Allah. You have a right over them, and of your right over them is that they do not allow anyone to tread upon your beds, nor should they disobey you in what is good. If they do that, then they are entitled to their provision and clothing in a good manner."

I say: That which clarifies the meaning of these narrations has already preceded.

In *al-Kafi* and *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَأَخَذْنَ مِنْكُمْ مِيثاقاً غَلِيظاً** — "...and they have taken from you a solemn covenant." He said: "The covenant (الميثاق) is the word by which the marriage was contracted."

In *al-Majma'*, he said: "The solemn covenant is the pledge taken from the husband at the time of the contract—to retain her honorably or release her kindly." He said: "And this is what is narrated from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him)."

I say: This meaning is narrated from several of the early exegetes such as Ibn Abbas, Qatadah, and Abu Mulaykah. The verse does not reject this, considering that it is a ruling that can truly be called a covenant taken from men for women, although it is more apparent that what is meant is the contract performed at the time of marriage.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, al-Zubayr ibn Bakkar extracted in *al-Muwaffaqiyyat* from Abdillah ibn Mus'ab who said: Umar said: "Do not increase the dowers of women beyond forty ounces (أوقية); whoever increases it, I shall cast the surplus into the public treasury (بيت المال)." A woman said: "That is not for you to do." He said: "And why?" She said: "Because Allah says: **وَآتَيْتُمْ إِحْداهُنَّ قِنْطاراً** — '...and you have given one of them a great amount [of wealth]...' (4:20)." So Umar said: "A woman is correct and a man has erred."

I say: It was also narrated by Abd al-Razzaq and Ibn al-Mundhir from Abd al-Rahman al-Sulami, and also by Sa'id ibn Mansur and Abu Ya'la with a good chain from Masruq; in it, "four hundred dirhams" is mentioned instead of "forty ounces." It was also narrated by Sa'id ibn Mansur and Abd ibn Humayd from Bakr ibn Abdillah al-Muzani. The narrations are close in meaning.

In the same source, Ibn Jarir extracted from Ikrimah regarding His saying: **وَلا تَنْكِحُوا ما نَكَحَ آباؤُكُمْ مِنَ النِّساءِ** — "Do not marry women whom your fathers married." He said: "It was revealed concerning Abu Qays ibn al-Aslat, who succeeded to Umm Ubayd bint Damrah who was under his father al-Aslat; and concerning al-Aswad ibn Khalaf who succeeded to the daughter of Abi Talhah ibn Abd al-Uzza ibn Uthman ibn Abd al-Dar, who was with his father Khalaf; and concerning Fakhtah daughter of al-Aswad ibn al-Muttalib ibn Asad, who was with Umayyah ibn Khalaf, so Safwan ibn Umayyah succeeded to her; and concerning Manzur ibn Rabab who succeeded to Mulaykah daughter of Kharijah, who was with his father Rabab ibn Sayyar."

In the same source, Ibn Sa'd extracted from Muhammad ibn Ka'b al-Qurazi who said: "When a man died leaving a wife, his son had more right to her to marry her if he wished—provided she was not his mother—or to marry her off to whomever he wished. When Abu Qays ibn al-Aslat died, his son Muhsin stood up and inherited his wife's marriage, but he did not spend on her nor did he give her anything from the inheritance. So she came to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and mentioned that to him. He said: 'Return, perhaps Allah will reveal something concerning you.' Then there was revealed: **وَلا تَنْكِحُوا ما نَكاَحَ آباؤُكُمْ مِنَ النِّساءِ** and there was revealed: **لا يَحِلُّ لَكُمْ أَنْ تَرِثُوا النِّساءَ كَرْهاً**."

I say: That which indicates this has already preceded from the narrations of the Shi'a.

In the same source, Ibn Jarir and Ibn al-Mundhir extracted from Ibn Abbas who said: "The people of the Age of Ignorance used to forbid what Allah forbade except for the father's wife and combining two sisters. So Allah revealed: **وَلا تَنْكِحُوا ما نَكَحَ آباؤُكُمْ مِنَ النِّساءِ** and **أَنْ تَجْمَعُوا بَيْنَ الْأُخْتَيْنِ** — '...and that you should marry two sisters together...'" (4:23). I say: There are other reports with the same meaning.
